Hey, this is Mark. Welcome back to Client Startup. Today we're going to go into session two of our example coaching sessions featuring Coach Magalie Pacea working with a young woman named Rebecca. Now to review quickly, this is an interesting session because it has a strong cultural component. The client, Rebecca, is a 22-year-old Canadian woman who converted to Islam during college and married a devout Muslim and her husband was raised in the Middle East. She's very interested in living her life according to the Islamic codes, particularly in being a good wife. Now Magalie had already asked a number of questions about Rebecca's family history, her relationships to the husband and family, and it seems that she's in a loving situation, so the outcome for the coaching sessions is to assist Rebecca in succeeding at her aspiration to be a good wife. Now pretty much every client has a word or phrase that's really meaningful to them, that encapsulates a great sense of purpose for them, and when you discover that word or phrase, it'll help you understand the client's attention and energy towards change. So for Rebecca, it's being a good wife. For someone else, it might be being the best, or it might be living with purpose, or it might be living the good life. Now you might want to use that phrase verbatim because you don't really want to paraphrase what that person's top aspiration is, but also when you help them clarify and prioritize their goals, what they want, their aspirations, you're also modifying and revising them so that they succeed at carrying them out. The right-wrong framework where everything is right and wrong, black and white, and where she's very critical of herself and constantly making herself wrong to a more better framework. It's not about being a right or wrong, it's about more better, being kind to herself, more creative, more able to succeed. When someone's suffering from an involuntary behavior or pattern that is clearly serving to meet a need, one strategy for changing that behavior is to prescribe the behavior deliberately, with a regular time and a regular procedure. This shifts the pattern's meaning because instead of being spontaneous, unconscious, or out of control, it becomes conscious, deliberate, and regulated. Also, since repeated behaviors and patterns serve to meet our needs, we're used to spending time and energy doing them. By prescribing those behaviors, you make sure that those needs are met in a controlled way. So in this case, Maggley prescribes using the doll to save Rebecca's skin, but also because the doll is something that you like, that you play with, that you take care of, that you treat with empathy. What do they really want in life? How do they want to get there? What ideas and people are an influence in their lives? What do they want? Who else wants it? And how do they expect to get there? Just try to put yourself in the space of what it would be like to walk that path to completing these goals. Fourth, check into the person's environment and relationships. If there are people in the person's life who may be contributing to their self-criticism, it would be good to know about it. What's the tone of these relationships? They may not be perfect, but are they basically supportive? Are they basically loving? Could they be called upon to support your client in their goals and aspirations? What are their needs? Remember the client's key relationships are more powerful force in their life than you are. You want to expand the unit so that you understand the people around your client, and so that you can help your client meet their needs as well. Fifth, empower. If the person's handicapped by criticisms and judgments, they need to be brought into an experience of acknowledging their strengths. This is where Maggley invited Rebecca to share what was extraordinary about herself. And Rebecca was probably saying these things for the first time in her life. They wrote a list of her extraordinary qualities, her strengths, and they backed them up by proof of the things that she's accomplished and chosen to do. Sixth, now that Rebecca is empowered by her strengths, we replace bad behaviors with good behaviors. Rebecca drives herself to achieve by criticizing herself, but then she breaks down in skin picking, which gives her an opportunity to connect and rest. So now instead of having bad days, she's going to have vacation days, which proactively allow her to connect with herself, rest, experience a variety, and reward herself for her hard work. And instead of occasionally erupting several times a week in skin picking and bad days, now she's going to pick her dull skin for 30 minutes every single day. These substitutions allow her to express the patterns that she already has, she's already used to doing it, but now in non-damaging ways.
